audit-01.text: What is the difference between governance and management in IT?
options: Governance sets direction and oversees; management executes within that direction
audit-01.explanation: Confusing the two is where audit failures begin — governance directs, management executes.

audit-01.text: In COBIT 2019, the EDM domain (Evaluate, Direct, Monitor) represents which layer?
options: The governance layer — board-level oversight of IT direction and outcomes
audit-01.explanation: EDM is COBIT's governance layer; APO/BAI/DSS/MEA are the management domains.

audit-01.text: How many governance and management domains does COBIT 2019 define?
options: Five — EDM for governance, plus APO, BAI, DSS, MEA for management
audit-01.explanation: COBIT 2019 has five domains: one governance (EDM) and four management.

audit-01.text: Which 2002 law formalized IT controls requirements for public companies after Enron?
audit-01.explanation: SOX §404 established IT controls requirements for public companies.

audit-01.text: What did the Enron collapse illustrate about IT governance?
options: Governance failures and lack of independent oversight enable catastrophic fraud
audit-01.explanation: Enron's governance breakdown triggered SOX and modern IT controls requirements.

audit-01.text: Is management's self-reporting to the board sufficient evidence that governance objectives are met?
options: No — auditors must independently verify both governance and management layers
audit-01.explanation: Independent verification is required; self-attestation isn't evidence.

audit-01.text: What COBIT capability level is the typical minimum for regulated industries?
audit-01.explanation: Level 3 (Established) is the usual regulated-industry minimum, not level 2.

audit-01.text: Why does confusing governance with management cause audit problems?
options: Oversight and execution blur, so no one independently checks direction is followed
audit-01.explanation: Separation of direction and execution is what makes oversight meaningful.

audit-02.text: What is the relationship between risk and controls?
options: Every control exists to mitigate a risk; without the risk, it's just overhead
audit-02.explanation: Controls are justified by the risks they reduce.

options: The risk remaining after existing controls reduce the inherent risk
audit-02.explanation: Residual = what remains after controls are applied to inherent risk.

options: Likelihood × Impact, before any controls are applied
audit-02.explanation: Inherent risk is raw exposure (L × I) with no controls factored in.

audit-02.text: Which is NOT one of the valid risk response options?
options: Ignore — defer the decision until the next audit
audit-02.explanation: The four responses are avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept — 'ignore' isn't one.

audit-02.text: Is 'Accept' valid when residual risk exceeds the board-approved risk appetite?
options: No — risks above appetite require a board-approved treatment plan
audit-02.explanation: Above-appetite risk can't be unilaterally accepted; it needs board treatment.

audit-02.text: What did the Capital One breach reveal about risk registers?
options: A stale/mis-scored register leaves critical risk treated as acceptable
audit-02.explanation: Misconfiguration risk was under-scored, leaving real exposure unmitigated.

audit-02.text: Why must a risk register be kept current?
options: Outdated scores hide real exposure and misdirect controls
audit-02.explanation: A stale register creates blind spots where risk is actually critical.

audit-02.text: Controls are applied to inherent risk to produce…
audit-02.explanation: Inherent risk minus the effect of controls = residual risk.

audit-03.text: How do IT General Controls (ITGCs) differ from application controls?
options: ITGCs apply across the whole IT environment and underpin all application controls
audit-03.explanation: Application controls mean nothing if the ITGCs beneath them are broken.

audit-03.text: Why is a single ITGC failure (e.g., emergency changes bypassing approval) so serious?
options: It's pervasive — it casts doubt on every report the affected system produced
audit-03.explanation: ITGC failures invalidate reliance on all application controls in that system.

audit-03.text: Which ITGC pillar ensures only authorized users can access systems and data?
options: Logical Access — least privilege and individual accountability
audit-03.explanation: Logical Access enforces who can reach systems, with accountability.

audit-03.text: Which is a Segregation of Duties (SoD) violation in change management?
options: The developer who wrote the code also approved their own change ticket
Two different people review a change
audit-03.explanation: Self-approval with no independent review is a classic SoD violation.

audit-03.text: What did the MF Global collapse demonstrate about ITGCs?
options: Multiple simultaneous ITGC failures together created the permissive environment for fraud
A single access failure alone caused it
audit-03.explanation: It was several concurrent ITGC failures, not one isolated gap.

audit-03.text: Why do auditors care so much about ITGCs in a financial audit?
options: If ITGCs fail, they can't rely on the system's financial data
audit-03.explanation: Reliable financial reporting depends on sound general controls.

audit-03.text: Logical access, change management, and operations are examples of…
Why test ITGCs before relying on application controls?
options: Application controls are only trustworthy if the general controls beneath them hold
audit-03.explanation: Weak ITGCs undermine every application control above them.

audit-04.text: What does the principle of least privilege mean?
options: Each user has exactly the permissions needed for their job, and nothing more
audit-04.explanation: Least privilege minimizes what any account can do or expose.

audit-04.text: How often must user access be reviewed/recertified under ISACA standards?
options: At least annually for all in-scope accounts
audit-04.explanation: Annual recertification is mandatory — it's not optional for non-privileged accounts.

audit-04.text: How quickly must privileged accounts be deprovisioned when an employee is terminated?
audit-04.explanation: Privileged access must be removed within 24 hours of termination.

audit-04.text: Which access-control failure does the Snowden case illustrate?
options: Privilege accumulation — access never removed after role changes — plus weak recertification
audit-04.explanation: Accumulated, un-reviewed access enabled mass data access.

audit-04.text: Is a shared admin account used by four people acceptable if the password is complex and rotated?
options: No — shared accounts provide no individual accountability or forensic attribution
audit-04.explanation: Shared accounts destroy accountability regardless of password strength.

audit-04.text: Why is 'access granted and never reviewed' dangerous?
options: It becomes permanent access regardless of role changes
audit-04.explanation: Without review, stale access accumulates into excess privilege.

audit-04.text: Why does each user need their own account?
options: So actions can be attributed to an individual for forensics and accountability
audit-04.explanation: Individual accounts enable attribution; shared ones don't.

audit-04.text: Periodically confirming each user still needs their access is called…
audit-04.explanation: Recertification re-validates access against current job needs.

audit-05.text: Why is change management a control?
options: Every production change is a controlled experiment — without control you're flying blind
It slows developers for no reason
audit-05.explanation: Controlled change reduces the risk that a deployment breaks production.

audit-05.text: Which ITIL change type requires Change Advisory Board (CAB) review before production?
audit-05.explanation: Normal changes go through CAB to assess risk and confirm testing.

audit-05.text: Why is an emergency-change rate above ~10% a red flag?
options: It signals teams are bypassing normal controls by labeling changes 'emergency'
It means the system is healthy
audit-05.explanation: High emergency rates indicate process circumvention, not real emergencies.

audit-05.text: What change-management failure caused Knight Capital's $440M loss?
options: A deployment not verified across all servers, with no automated rollback
audit-05.explanation: A partial deployment caused erroneous trades with no rollback to stop them.

audit-05.text: When is a rollback plan required?
options: For all production changes — if you can't undo it, you can't approve it
audit-05.explanation: A rollback plan is mandatory for every production change.

audit-05.text: A change deployed out-of-window by the same person who developed it represents how many control violations?
options: Two — a Segregation of Duties failure AND an out-of-window deployment
audit-05.explanation: SoD failure and out-of-window deployment are two separate violations.

audit-05.text: Deploying outside the approved maintenance window is…
options: A control violation — changes must occur in the approved window
Only an issue if it fails
audit-05.explanation: Out-of-window deployment bypasses the agreed change control.

audit-05.text: What does change management primarily protect against?
options: Untested or unauthorized changes breaking production
audit-05.explanation: It ensures changes are tested, approved, and reversible.

audit-06.text: Why is an untested recovery plan not a real control?
options: A plan never tested is a hope — only tested recovery with documented results counts
audit-06.explanation: ISACA requires test evidence with results for a DR plan to be a control.

audit-06.text: What does RPO (Recovery Point Objective) define?
options: The maximum data loss the business can accept — driving backup frequency
audit-06.explanation: RPO sets the acceptable data-loss window, which drives backup cadence.

audit-06.text: Is an RTO of 2 hours acceptable when the Maximum Tolerable Downtime (MTD) is also 2 hours?
options: No — RTO must be less than MTD to leave margin for complications
audit-06.explanation: RTO must be below MTD; equal leaves no buffer.

audit-06.text: What DR design flaw did Hurricane Katrina expose at New Orleans institutions?
options: Primary and backup data centers were in the same flood zone — both lost at once
audit-06.explanation: Co-located primary and backup sites failed together.

audit-06.text: A DR test shows actual RTO of 5h20m against a 4-hour target. The auditor's finding?
options: A HIGH finding — the plan doesn't meet its approved RTO; remediation required
audit-06.explanation: Missing the approved RTO is a high finding even if MTD isn't breached.

options: The target time to restore a system after disruption
audit-06.explanation: RTO is the restoration-time target; RPO is the data-loss target.

audit-06.text: Why must primary and backup data centers be geographically separated?
options: So a single regional disaster can't take out both at once
audit-06.explanation: Separation prevents one event from destroying both sites — the Katrina lesson.

audit-06.text: What turns a DR plan into a control an auditor can rely on?
options: A successful test with documented results
audit-06.explanation: Documented successful tests are the evidence that makes it a control.

audit-07.text: Why is an IR plan that was never exercised not a real control?
options: An untested plan sitting in a folder won't work under pressure — exercises make it a control
It's a control if it's long
audit-07.explanation: An incident response plan must be exercised to count as effective.

audit-07.text: Which NIST SP 800-61 IR phase is most auditable?
options: Preparation — plans, role assignments, tabletop records, contact lists are verifiable
audit-07.explanation: Preparation produces the most documentary evidence to verify.

audit-07.text: Why did Equifax fail to detect the breach for 76 days?
options: Its SSL inspection tool's certificate had been expired for 19 months
audit-07.explanation: An expired cert blinded monitoring — a control failure, not a novel exploit.

audit-07.text: An IR plan last updated 21 months ago with 3 overdue tabletop action items rates as…
options: INEFFECTIVE — currency and follow-through both failed
audit-07.explanation: Annual update and action-item follow-through are both requirements.

audit-07.text: What does Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) measure?
options: Time from when the incident occurred to when it was detected

audit-07.text: Is it acceptable to have no ransomware playbook if the org has never had a ransomware incident?
options: No — playbooks must cover the primary threat landscape regardless of past incidents
audit-07.explanation: Absence of a relevant playbook is itself a finding.

audit-07.text: The Equifax detection failure shows that security tools…
options: Must be maintained (e.g., cert renewals) or they silently stop working
audit-07.explanation: An unmaintained tool is a decorative checkbox, not a control.

audit-07.text: NIST SP 800-61 is the standard framework for…
audit-07.explanation: 800-61 defines the incident-handling lifecycle and phases.

audit-08.text: What is the core supply-chain risk principle?
options: Your security is only as strong as the weakest vendor with access to your systems
audit-08.explanation: Third-party access extends your attack surface.

options: Access to sensitive data/critical systems, often single-source, needing the highest assessment frequency
audit-08.explanation: Tier 1 vendors carry the most risk and need the most scrutiny.

audit-08.text: Is a questionnaire-only assessment enough for a Tier 1 vendor with admin access?
options: No — Tier 1 needs SOC 2 Type II (or equivalent) plus contractual right-to-audit
Only a phone call is needed
audit-08.explanation: Critical vendors require evidence-based assurance, not self-reported forms.

audit-08.text: Why did the SolarWinds supply-chain attack succeed at so many orgs?
options: They failed to classify SolarWinds as a critical vendor needing enhanced monitoring
It used a zero-day in browsers
audit-08.explanation: Trusted vendor software wasn't monitored as the critical dependency it was.

audit-08.text: What's required when a Tier 1 vendor's annual assessment lapses beyond 365 days?
options: Suspend the vendor's access until a current assessment is completed
audit-08.explanation: Continued access with a lapsed assessment is an unmitigated risk.

audit-08.text: When must a vendor's access be deprovisioned?
options: When the relationship ends — not at the next scheduled review
audit-08.explanation: Access must be removed at relationship end, immediately.

audit-08.text: A lesson from SolarWinds for critical vendors is to…
options: Monitor the vendor's software network activity, not just trust its updates
audit-08.explanation: Enhanced monitoring of trusted software can catch supply-chain compromise.

audit-08.text: Why are vendors with system access in audit scope?
options: Their access and security posture directly affect your risk
audit-08.explanation: Vendor risk is your risk when they can touch your systems.

audit-09.text: Why is collecting data you don't need a liability?
options: Unneeded data is risk to store and protect, not an asset
audit-09.explanation: Excess data expands breach impact and compliance burden.

audit-09.text: What does GDPR Article 5(1)(e) (storage limitation) require?
options: Delete personal data once it's no longer needed, per the retention schedule
audit-09.explanation: Data must not be kept longer than necessary for its purpose.

audit-09.text: Under GDPR, how quickly must a breach be reported to the supervisory authority?
audit-09.explanation: GDPR requires authority notification within 72 hours of discovery.

audit-09.text: A table kept 450,000 records for 3,353 days against a 730-day policy violates what — and the action?
options: Article 5(1)(e) storage limitation — delete records older than 730 days and notify the DPO
audit-09.explanation: Over-retention breaches storage limitation; purge and notify the DPO.

audit-09.text: Meta's €1.2B GDPR fine in 2023 was for…
options: Transferring EU personal data to US servers after the legal basis was invalidated
audit-09.explanation: Invalid EU-US data transfers drove the record fine.

audit-09.text: Under GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure), a deletion request must be fulfilled…
options: Within 30 days, removing data from all systems including accessible backups
audit-09.explanation: Erasure covers all stores the data can be accessed from, including backups.

options: Collect and keep only the data actually needed for a stated purpose
audit-09.explanation: Less data held means less to leak and less to govern.

audit-09.text: Why do organizations need documented retention schedules?
options: To prove data is deleted when no longer needed, satisfying storage limitation
audit-09.explanation: Schedules operationalize and evidence the storage-limitation principle.

audit-10.text: What single thing separates a penetration tester from a criminal hacker?
options: Written authorization from the system owner for the systems and tests performed
audit-10.explanation: Authorization is the legal protection that makes testing lawful.

audit-10.text: If a cloud workload is within the authorized IP range, is more approval needed before testing?
options: Yes — cloud providers (AWS/Azure/GCP) each have their own pentest approval policies
audit-10.explanation: Cloud workloads require explicit provider authorization too.

audit-10.text: A tester scans an explicitly out-of-scope core banking subnet. What must happen?
options: Document it, disclose to the client within 24 hours, and cease testing immediately
audit-10.explanation: Out-of-scope access must be stopped, documented, and disclosed.

audit-10.text: What did the 2010 AT&T iPad incident illustrate?
options: Without written authorization, access — even with defensive intent — can be prosecuted
audit-10.explanation: Good intentions don't substitute for authorization.

audit-10.text: Does listing only authorized IPs (without listing out-of-scope systems) adequately protect the tester?
options: No — out-of-scope systems must be explicitly listed; ambiguity protects no one
audit-10.explanation: Explicit out-of-scope listings remove dangerous ambiguity.

audit-10.text: Whose signature makes a pentest authorization valid?
options: The system owner authorizing the specific systems and test types
audit-10.explanation: The owner must authorize the defined scope and test types.

audit-10.text: Why are clear rules of engagement essential before testing?
options: They define what's permitted, protecting both client and tester legally
audit-10.explanation: Defined scope and ROE keep the engagement lawful and bounded.

audit-10.text: The document that authorizes and bounds a penetration test is the…
audit-10.explanation: Scope/authorization defines and legally protects the engagement.

audit-11.text: Why does an audit finding require evidence?
options: An assertion without evidence is just an opinion — evidence makes a finding valid
audit-11.explanation: Evidence is what makes an audit conclusion defensible.

audit-11.text: What three attributes make audit evidence adequate?
audit-11.explanation: Evidence must be enough, credible, and actually address the control tested.

audit-11.text: In attribute sampling, if the sample deviation rate exceeds the tolerable rate, the auditor must…
options: Conclude the control is not operating effectively (or expand testing)
audit-11.explanation: Exceeding the tolerable rate means the control fails the test.

audit-11.text: 5 of 60 change tickets lack CAB approval; tolerable rate is 5%. The conclusion?
options: 8.3% deviation exceeds 5% — the change-management control is not operating effectively

audit-11.text: Which is true about audit evidence quality?
options: Documentary evidence ranks higher than testimonial; directly observed physical evidence is highest

audit-11.text: What principle did the Arthur Andersen case establish about evidence?
options: Evidence must be preserved (≈7 years); destroying it is obstruction of justice
Evidence can be shredded after review
audit-11.explanation: Collection isn't enough — evidence must be retained, not destroyed.

options: The maximum error rate at which the auditor can still rely on the control
audit-11.explanation: It's the threshold beyond which the control is deemed ineffective.

audit-11.text: What elevates an audit finding from opinion to fact?
options: Sufficient, reliable, relevant evidence supporting it
audit-11.explanation: Quality evidence is what substantiates a finding.

audit-12.text: Why is the audit report described as 'the product'?
options: It communicates the findings and risk — everything before it is just the work
audit-12.explanation: The report is what conveys value and drives remediation.

audit-12.text: What four elements make a complete CISA audit finding?
audit-12.explanation: Condition/Criteria/Cause/Effect: what was found, the standard, why, and the risk.

audit-12.text: How should a repeat finding (unchanged from a prior audit) be handled?
options: Escalate directly to the audit committee — management accountability has failed
audit-12.explanation: Repeat findings go straight to the audit committee.

audit-12.text: Tracking remediation at 30, 60, and 90 days after the report is best described as…
options: An audit control itself — untracked remediation is indistinguishable from none
audit-12.explanation: Without tracking, remediation can't be verified to have happened.

audit-12.text: What audit risk does the Wells Fargo case illustrate?
options: Management agrees to remediation plans but fails to implement them — producing repeat findings
It was a single one-time error
audit-12.explanation: Acknowledged-but-unimplemented findings signal a broken control environment.

audit-12.text: When is a current finding a 'repeat finding'?
options: Same condition and control as a prior-year finding, not remediated in 12+ months
A finding about a different system
audit-12.explanation: Same unremediated condition across cycles makes it a repeat.

audit-12.text: Why are repeat findings escalated to the audit committee rather than re-issued to management?
options: Management has demonstrably failed to act, so higher oversight is required
audit-12.explanation: Repeat failure is an accountability problem for governance to address.

audit-12.text: What completes the audit lifecycle after the report is issued?
options: Tracked remediation of the findings to verified closure
audit-12.explanation: Verified remediation closes the loop; untracked remediation isn't a control.
